text: COLINE'E, or Colline'E, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Côtes-du- Nord, arrond. of Loudéac. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,812; in 1841, 6,888. The town is 16 m. NE of Loudéac, on the Montagne-du- Menez. Pop. 573.
COLINSBURGH, a village in the p. of Kilconquhar, in Fifeshire, on the Great coast road. Pop. 482. There is a large currying work here.

COLINTON, or Collington, a parish at the base of the Pentland hills, in Mid Lothian, SW of Edinburgh. The surface is beautifully varied, descending from the N range of the Pentlands towards the plain of Corstorphine, in diversified and occasionally bold undulations. Along its southern limit the Pentlands rise in the different summits 1,450, 1,550, and 1,700 ft. above sea-level; and toward the NE are the picturesque heights of the Fir hill and Craig-Lockhart hill. Over a distance of 3 m. the p. is intersected by the water of Leith. Three rivulets or rills also enrich it with their waters, -Murray-burn, Braid-burn, COL and Burdiehouse-burn. The village of C. is situated on the water of Leith, near the centre of the p., and is the site of several extensive paper manufactories. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 1,397; in 1841, 2,195. Pop. of the v. 120. This p. likewise contains the villages of Hailes-Quarry, Juniper-Green, Slateford, and Swanston.
COLIUMO, a little port on the coast of Chili, close to the N of Concepcion bay, in S lat. 36° 31' 30", W long. 73° 01′ 15".
COLKIRK, a parish of Norfolk, 2 m. S of Fakenham. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 462.

COLL, one of the Western isles annexed to Argyleshire, and the p. of Tiree. It lies off the W coast of Mull, and is divided from Tiree by a narrow sound. It is about 14 m. in length, and 24 m. in breadth; containing 9,999 acres. Two-thirds of this extent are hills, rocks, shifting-sands, lakes, and morasses; the other third is pasture, meadow, or corn land. Its surface is diversified with eminences, and covered with a thin stratum of earth. In many places a grey, stony surface, without herbage of any kind, presents itself to the eye; but in other quarters the sandy soil is covered, during spring and summer, with an enamelled carpet of brilliant and odorous plants. C. abounds with shallow lakes, of which several contain trouts and eels. Black cattle are fed on the island, and about 200 are annually exported. The inhabitants employ themselves chiefly in fishing and agriculture. Pop. in 1801, 1,162; in 1841, 1,442.
COLLACE, a parish in Perthshire, lying on the N declivity of the Sidlaw hills, and containing the villages of C., Saucher, and Kinrossie. Pop. in 1801, 562; in 1841, 702.
COLLADO - DE - CONTRERAS, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Avila, partido of Arévalo, 3 m. SE of Fontiveros.
COLLADO-MEDIANO, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 27 m. NW of Madrid, near the r. bank of the Manzanares.
COLLADO-VILLALBA, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. NNW of Madrid.
COLLALTO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 9 m. N of Udino.
COLLARASS, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 80 m. N of Seronge, in N lat. 25° 14'.

COLLARES, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 21 m. WNW of Lisbon, in a beautiful and fertile valley on the Rio-das-Macas. Pop. 2,200. -Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. and 30 m. NNE of Para.
COLLE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, district and 15 m. SSE of Campobasso. Pop. 4,361.
COLLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Vence. Pop. 1,551.
COLLE-D'ANCHISE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, district and 7 m. SW of Campobasso. Pop. 1,768.
COLLE-SALVETTI, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 10 m. SE of Pisa. Pop. 809.

COLLE-DI-VAL-D'ELSA, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 12 m. NW of Sienna, on the l. bank of the Elsa, at an alt. of 623 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,573. It is the see of a bishop. It has extensive paper-mills, and manufactories of glass, crystal, and pottery.
COLLEDA, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the district of Merseburg, on the river Losse. Pop. 2,700.
COLLEGE, a village in Knox co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. 232.
COLLEGE-CORNERS, a township in Butler co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 110 m. from Columbus.
COLLEGE-HILL, a village of Lafayette co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 166 m. NNE of Jackson.
COLLEGEVILLE, a village in Saline co., in the state of Arkansas, U. S., 15 m. SW of Little Rock.
COLLEGIO, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Alagoas, on the l. bank of the Sao-Francisco, 35 m. above its mouth, and 17 m. from Penedo.
COLLEGIO, a town of Modena, in the gov. and 9 m. NE of Modena, on the l. bank of the Panaro.
COLLERET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Maubeuge. Pop. 1,014.
COLLES, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 20 m. SW of Ourique.
COLLESANO, a town of Sicily, in the district and 9 m. SW of Cefalu, near the source of the Roccella. Pop. 3,000.

COLLESSIE, a parish and village of Fifeshire, on the N side of 'the Howe,' or central valley of the co., skirted by the river Eden. Pop. in 1801, 930; in 1841, 1,346, of whom 210 were in the v.—It contains also the villages of Edenton, Giffordton, Kinloch, Ladybank, and Monkston.
COLLET-DE-DE'ZE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lozère, cant. of St. Germain-de-Calberte, 22 m. from Florac. Pop. in 1841, 1,329. Antimony is wrought in the environs.

COLLETON, a district of the state of South Carolina, U. S., comprising an area of 2,100 sq. m., bounded on the SW by the Combahee river, on the SE by the Atlantic, and intersected by the Edesto. Its principal productions are rice and cotton. Pop. in 1830, 27,256; in 1840, 25,478. Its cap. is Waterboro.
COLLETORTO, or Colle Torto, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, district and 12 m. SSE of Larino, on a hill. Pop. 3,805.
COLLERYN, a township in the p. of Llansaintffraid, Montgomeryshire. Pop. 182.

COLLI, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 23 m. NW of Piedimonte, on a hill bathed by the Volturno. Pop. 1,313. COLLI (GLI),—the ancient Ereta,—a hamlet of Sicily, in the district and 3 m. NNW of Palermo, on a mountain of the same name.
COLLIE, a river of Western Australia, in Wellington co., which descends from Mount Lennard in Roe's Range, runs NW and WSW, and after uniting with Preston river flows into Port Leschenault.
COLLIE (CAPE), a headland on the coast of Russian America, in N lat. 70° 37′ 24″, W long. 159° 55' 24".

COLLIER BAY, an extensive indentation of the NW coast of Australia, ESE of Buccaneer archipelago, intersected by the parallel of 16° 20' S, and the meridian of 124° 15' E. It is 20 m. wide at its entrance. Spring-tides fall 36 ft. here, leaving the greater part of the bay dry at low water.
COLLIERLEY, a township in the p. of Lanchester, co. and 11 m. NW of Durham, near a branch of the Derwent. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 853.
COLLIER'S-END, a hamlet in the p. of Standon, Hertfordshire. Pop. 233.
COLLIER'S-ROCK, a rock about 50 ft. in circuit, and 6 or 7 ft. above water, 2 m. NW from Hog point, the NE limit of Sunda strait, in S lat. 5° 54'.
COLLIESTON, a fishing-village of Aberdeenshire, in the p. of Slains. Pop. 167.
COLLIERSTOWN, a village of Rockbridge co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 155 m. NW of Richmond, on Collier creek.
COLLIGAN, a parish in co. Waterford, 31 m. NNW of Dungarvan. Area 3,784 acres. Pop. Pop. 1,049. 1,084.
COLLIN, a village in the p. of Torthorwald, in Dumfries-shire, near the Dumfries and Carlisle road. Pop. 582.
COLLINGBOURNE-DUCIS, a parish of Wilts, Ardee, and 34 m. N by W of Dublin. Pop. 936. COL 21 m. NNW of Ludgershall. Area 3,570 acres. Pop. 518.
COLLINGBOURNE - KINGSTON, a parish of Wilts, 4 m. NNW of Ludgershall. Area 7,160 acres. Pop. 933.
COLLINGHAM, a parish of the W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SW of Wetherby, on the S bank of the Warfe. Area 2,200 acres. Pop. 324.
COLLINGHAM (NORTH), a parish of Nottinghamshire, 51 m. NNE of Newark, on a branch of the Trent. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 911.
COLLINGHAM (South), a parish of Nottinghamshire, 43 m. NNE of Newark. Area 3,220 acres. Pop. 721.
COLLINGTON, a parish of Herefordshire, 3\frac{1}{4} m. N of Bromyard. Area 1,120 acres. Pop. 160.
COLLINGTREE, a parish of Northamptonshire, 31 m. S of Northampton, near the London and Birmingham railway. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 232.

COLLINS, a township of Erie co., in the state of New York, U. S., 312 m. W of Albany, drained by Cattaraugus creek and its tributaries. Its surface is undulating, and its soil varies in quality. Pop. in 1840, 4,257.
COLLINSTOWN, a village in the p. of Kilcumney, co. Westmeath, 31 m. NW of Castletown-Delvin. Pop. in 1831, 145.

COLLINSVILLE, a village of Canton township, Hartford co., in the state of Connecticut, U. S., 15 m. NW of Hartford, on both sides of Farmington river. Pop. in 1840, about 1,000.—Also a village of West Turin township, Lewis co., in the same state, 123 m. NW of Albany. Pop. 130.—Also a village of Madison co., in the state of Illinois, 86 m. SW of Springfield.

COLLIO, or Colio, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 20 m. NNE of Brescia, in the Val-Trompio, on the r. bank of the Mella. Pop. 1,800. The environs contain mines of iron, and a quarry of green marble.

COLLIOURE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Argeles, 21 m. E of Ceret, situated on the brow of a hill, on the shore of the Mediterranean. Pop. in 1836, 3,274; in 1841, 3,476. The town is surrounded by old walls, and is defended by a strong castle. Good wines, known as Vins de Rancio and Vins de Grenache, are manufactured here; and a considerable commerce is conducted from the small port of C. in sardines, anchovies, and wines. The total exports in 1841 amounted to 8,127 quintals; and the tonnage of the port consisted of 77 vessels = 445 tons.

COLLO, or Colo [Arab. Kolla or Koallu], a town of Algeria, in the prov. of Constantine, 75 m. W of Bona, on a small bay of the same name, in N lat. 37° 2', E long. 6° 65'. It is inhabited by about 2,000 Arabs, who conduct an active coasting-trade. The environs present a level plain covered with rich vegetation, and in the centre of which rises an isolated conical mountain covered with wood, called Roumadiah. This town was taken possession of by the French in April 1843.

COLLOBRIE'RES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Toulon.— The cant., comprising 2 com., had a pop. of 3,584 in 1841.—The com., 20 m. ENE of Toulon, had a pop. of 1,866.
COLLODI, a town of the duchy of Lucca, 9 m. ENE of Lucca, on the l. bank of the Pescia-Minore.

COLLON, a parish partly in co. Meath, partly in co. Louth. Area in Meath 2,045 acres; in Louth 6,768. Pop. of the Meath section 540; of the Louth 2,735.—The market-town of C. is 51 m. S by E of It is the neatest and most beautiful place of its class in Ireland; and owes the greater part of its attractions to the taste and liberality of the late Lord Oriel. The main street is edificed with houses in the old English style of architecture. The market-house occupies three sides of a small square. Even the cottages or cabins, in the poorest parts of the town, are, in most instances, white-washed and roofed with slate. The church consists of three parallel aisles, surmounted by three spires; and is a handsome structure, in imitation of the ancient English style of architecture. A small stream passes through the town, and is spanned by a stone-bridge. A cotton manufactory and a bleaching-green were established to provide profitable scope for the industry of the inhabitants; and stocking-making and linen-weaving employ a considerable number of families.

COLLON (COL-DE-), a pass in the Pennine Alps, at the head of the Val-Pellina, or rather at the head of the first lateral valley on the left, to those ascending, below the head of the Val-Pellina. It has an alt. of 10,333 ft.; and is said to be occasionally traversed by smugglers, who introduce into the Piedmontese valleys, by these less-frequented passes, various contraband articles which are of free commerce in Switzerland.
COLLONG. See CALLAM.
COLLONGE, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, on the r. bank of the Rhone, opposite Saint-Maurice. Pop. 375.

COLLONGES, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Gex.—The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. in 1841 of 9,339. —The com., 18 m. from Gex, had a pop. of 1,256.— Also a com. in the dep. of Corrèze, 10 m. SE of Brives. Pop. 1,250.

COLLONSAY, one of the Hebrides, belonging to Argyleshire. It is separated from Oronsay only by a narrow sound, which is dry at low water. The two islands lie nearly 9 m. N by W of the N extremity of Islay; and from the S end of Oronsay to the N end of Collonsay, are 12 m. long, and from 1 to 3 m. broad. The surface is unequal, rising into a considerable number of rugged hills covered with heath. They contain about 9,000 acres, of which 3,000 are arable. The soil is light; along the shore it inclines to sand, producing early and tolerable crops. "The first sight of C. is very unpromising, and would not lead a traveller to expect the fertile and extensive valleys which he meets with in traversing the island. Although there are no hills of any consequence, or which exceed an elevation of 800 ft. above the level of the sea, yet their tops are bare and weather-beaten, and convey the idea of hopeless barrenness and desolation. The dip of the rock is from SW to NE, as is very often the case in the adjacent isles." [Macdonald's General View of the Hebrides. London, 1811, 8vo.] The breed of cattle is excellent. Near the centre of C. is a fresh water loch called Loch Fad. Pop. of the two islands in 1801, 805; in 1831, 893. These islands are parochially connected with Jura.
COLLOONEY, a town in the p. of Ballysadere, co. Sligo, on the river Owenbeg, 13 m. S by E of Ballysadere. Pop. 651.
COLLOREC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Finistère, 15 m. ENE of Chateaulon. Pop. 1,308.
COLLUMBKILL, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 1½ m. NE of Thomastown. Area 4,473 acres. Pop. 1,116. -Also a p. in co. Longford, 3 m. W of Granard. Area 20,313 acres. Pop. 9,273.

COLLUMPTON, a market-town and parish in the co. of Devon, 12 m. NE of Exeter, and 160 m. S by W of London, on the river Culm, in a large valley surrounded by an extensive plain. The Exeter | lencia. COL and Bristol railway has a station here 1814 m. from London, 651 m. from Plymouth, and 121 m. from Exeter. Pop. in 1801, 3,138; in 1841, 3,909. The town consists chiefly of one long ill-paved street, stretching along the great road between Exeter and Bath. The church—a fine structure, having a nave, chancel, 3 aisles, and a lofty tower at the W end-is the only ornament of which the town can boast. Its interior is richly carved and decorated. There were in this town at the beginning of the present cent. 40 manufacturers, small capitalists, chiefly in the serge flannel trade. The number of looms still employed, but chiefly by one house, is nearly 500, some of which are in the town, and the remainder in the adjacent villages. Since the expiration of the charter of the East India company, who purchased the serges of C. for their China trade, this branch of manufacture has been very fluctuating; but it is supposed that the Portuguese market is now supplied with C. serge instead of Salisbury flannel.—This town was devastated by fire in the month of July, 1839, when upwards of 170 buildings were totally destroyed. It is one of the polling-places for the N division of the county.
COLLY-WESTON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 4 m. from Stamford. Area 1,690 acres. Pop. 434.

COLMAR, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin.-The arrond. has an area of 168,856 hectares. Pop. in 1841, 202,557. It comprises the 13 cantons of Andolsheim, C., Ensisheim, Guebwiller, Kaysersberg, Sainte - Marie - aux - Mines, Munster, Neuf-Brisach, Poutroye, Ribeauville, Rouflach, Soultz, and Wintzenheim.—The cant. had a pop. of 21,517 in 1841. It comprises 2 com.—The com. and town are situated on the l. or W bank of the Lauch, at the confluence of the Fecht, in N lat. 48° 4' 41", E long. 7° 21' 22", 234 m. ESE of Paris, and 41 m. SSW of Strasburg; at an alt. of 652 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1789, 11,960; in 1841, 19,908. It is a well-built town, the seat of a court-of-appeal, a communal college, a normal school, and a Protestant consistory. It has a public library of 40,000 vols. Its chief manufactures are cottons, printed goods, silk ribbons, and pâtes-defoies-d'oie. It also conducts a considerable trade in iron, grain, wine, and the agricultural productions of the surrounding district.—The site of this town has been identified by some antiquaries with that of the Roman Columbaria. It was rebuilt by Frederic II. in 1220, and was erected into an imperial city in 1552. It was taken by the Swedes in 1632; in 1648 it was ceded to France by the treaty of Munster; and in 1673 its fortifications were destroyed by order of Louis XIV.

COLMARS, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, arrond. of Castellane.—The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. 4,076. The town is 28 m. N of Castellane, on the l. bank of the Verdon, on the frontiers of Sardinia. It is fortified. Pop. 1,033.
COLMENAR, a village and partido of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. N or Malaga, between the Gordo and the Guadalmedina.
COLMENAR-DEL-ARROYO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 39 m. SE of Avila, on an affluent of the Perales.
COLMENAR-DE-OREJA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. SSE of Madrid. Pop. 2,500. Limestone is wrought in the neighbourhood of this town.
COLMENAR-DE-LA-SIERRA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 39 m. N of Guadalaxara, on the L bank of the Jarama.
COLMENAREJO, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. NW of Madrid, near the l. bank of the Au-

COLMENAR-VIEJO, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. N of Madrid, between the Manzanares and the Tejada. Pop. 5,000. Coarse woollens are made here. Much of the granite used for building in Madrid is quarried in the neighbourhood of this town.
COLMOLYN, or CULMULLEN, a parish in co. Meath. Area 5,565 acres. Pop. 1,010. The hamlet of C., 3 m. SW of Dunshaughlin, is a small place.

COLMONELL, a large parish in Ayrshire, skirting the frith of Clyde from Girvan to Ballantrae. It is 193 m. in length, with an average breadth of 6 m. Pop. in 1801, 1,306; in 1841, 2,801. The village of C., on the N bank of the Stinchar, 5 m. above Ballantrae, has a pop. of about 300.
COLMWORTH, a parish in Bedfordshire, 5 m. W by S of St. Neot's. Area 2,310 acres. Pop. 575.
COLN, or Cold-St.-Aldwyn's, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 3 m. N of Fairford, on the river Coln. Area 3,420 acres. Pop. 428.
COLN-ST.-DENNIS, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 3 m. SSW of North Leach, on the river Coln. Area 2,430 acres. Pop. 200.

COLNBROOK, a chapelry, and formerly a market-town, partly in the p. of Stanwell, Middlesex, and partly in the parishes of Horton-Iver and Langley- Marsh, Buckingham, 17 m. SW of London. Pop. 1,050. This town, which is a place of great antiquity, stands upon the small river Coln, by four branches or streams of which it is intersected, and over each of which it has a small bridge, -a circumstance which Camden thinks decisive proof that it is the Pontes of Antoninus.
COLN-ROGERS, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 4 m. S by W of North Leach. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 137.

COLNE, a river which originates in the union of several small streams in the co. of Hertford, and running through that county and Middlesex, falls, by several channels, into the Thames, principally at Staines. The Grand Junction canal follows the course of this river from Rickmansworth in Hertfordshire till it leaves that county. It has a total course of 40 m. Its tributaries are the Muse, the Gade, and the Chess.—Also a river in Essex, which rises near Ridgewell, on the NW side of the county, and, flowing E and SE, after passing Colchester, expands into a wide estuary, navigable to the German ocean. It has a total course of 35 m.—Also a river in Gloucestershire, which rises in the interior of the county, and flowing SE, falls into the Isis near Lechlade, on the borders of Oxfordshire.
COLNE, a parish in the co. of Huntingdon, 11 m. S by E of Somersham. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 544.

COLNE, a chapelry and market-town in the p. of Whalley, co.-palatine of Lancaster, 35 m. SE of Lancaster, on the N bank of the river Henburn. Area of c. 8,050 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,626; in 1831, 8,080; in 1841, 8,615. The town-which is of great antiquity-stands upon a point of rising ground between the Leeds and Liverpool canal, and the Calder. Its manufactures formerly consisted in woollen and worsted goods. The cotton trade now prevails here. Calicoes, dimities, and mousselins-de-laine, are manufactured. Several spinning and weaving mills have of late years been erected here. There is a cloth or piece hall in this town, built on the plan of that in Leeds. Lying near the rich pastures of Craven, considerable business is done at C. in the sale of cattle. The last Wednesday of every month is a kind of extra market or fair-day. The country in the vicinity is hilly, and abounds with coal, stone, limestone, slate, &c., for which a ready transit is afforded by the Leeds and Liverpool canal, which passes, at its summit-level, through a tunnel about 1 m. from the town. COL -C. is supposed to have been the Colunio of the anonymous Ravennas.
COLNE-EARLS, or GREAT COLNE, a parish in Essex, 31 m. SE of Halstead. Area 2,910 acres. Pop. 1,385.
COLNE-ENGAIN, or LITTLE COLNE, a parish in Essex, 21 m. E of Halstead. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 685.
COLNE-WAKE, a parish in Essex, 54 m. ESE of Halstead, on the river Colne. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 444.
COLNE-WHITE, a parish in Essex, 42 m. E of Halstead. Area 1,730 acres. Pop. 110.
COLNETT (CAPE), a headland on the coast of New California, in N lat. 31° 15'.
COLNEY, a parish in Norfolk, 31 m. E of Norwich, on the S bank of the river Yare. Area 750 acres. Pop. 110.
COLOCYTHIA. See KOLOKYTHIA.
COLOCZA. See KALOCSA.
COLOGNA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 20 m. SE of Verona, at an alt. of 574 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1837, 6,000. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.

COLOGNE, or Koln, a government or administrative subdivision of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine. It has an area of 1,614 sq. m., and is divided into the 11 circles of Wipperfurt, Gummersbach, Waldbröl, Sieg-Kreis, Müllheim, the Stadt-kreis- Köln, the Land-kreis-Köln, Bergheim, Euskirchen, Rheinbach, and Bonn. It comprises 12 towns. Pop. in 1837, 418,481; of whom 357,402 were Catholics, and 56,468 Protestants. Pop. in 1846, 484,593.

COLOGNE, or KOLN, the capital of the above prov., once the capital of an archbishopric and electorate,-afterwards subject to France, and included in the dep. of the Roer,—is an ancient city, built in the form of a crescent on the l. or W bank of the Rhine, in N lat. 50° 58', E long. 6° 53'; at an alt. of 121 ft. above sealevel. The river is here 1,300 ft. wide, and from 25 to 50 ft. deep. On account of the wealth, number, and splendour of its clergy, and the variety of its churches, chapels, monasteries, nunneries, and relics, with their legends and miracles, C. has been denominated 'the Rome of Germany.' The pop. in 1827 was 57,022; in 1837, 66,179; in 1846 it was returned along with that of Deutz at 95,202. It stretches 21/2 m. in length, from N to S, and about 1 m. inland, and is enclosed by a lofty wall nearly 6 m. in circuit, with ramparts and ditches. The streets are narrow; and owing to the great elevation of the houses are also gloomy. With the decline of the Hanseatic league, to which it belonged, this city lost its riches; and, under the French government, its opulent clergy, and beautiful works of art. The great warehouses of its former merchants are still standing as monuments of the past, but only a small number of the new buildings are distinguished for beauty. The handsomest public places are the new market with its lime trees, the hay-market, and the old market. One of the noblest works of Gothic architecture is the unfinished cathedral, in the form of a cross, 400 ft. long, and 180 wide. It was in the course of erection from the year 1284 until the Reformation; at which juncture only the choir, 180 ft. high, with the chapels around it, had been completed. The nave is supported by 100 columns, of which the four central ones are 40 ft. in circumf.; but it has only two-thirds of its intended height, and is still covered with a wooden roof. Each of the towers was designed to be 500 ft. high; 250 ft. of one is finished, and only 21 ft. of the other. Considerable progress is now making towards completing this unrivalled edifice. The church of St. Gereon has a lofty dome and three galleries. The church of St. Cunibert has an altar like the famous altar of St. Peter's church in i Rome. The church of St. Peter has an admirable painting, by Rubens, of the martyrdom of the apostle Peter. The town-house has a splendid portico, adorned with two rows of marble columns. The Jesuits' library, though it has been deprived of many works, still contains 60,000 volumes. The city still contains some beautiful collections of works of art. C. is the see of an archbishop, whose suffragans are the bishops of Treves, Munster, Paderborn, and Hildesheim. It is also the seat of an Appellations-Gericht- Hof, or royal court of appeal for those parts of Prussia which use the French law. It has an evangelical gymnasium with 12 professors and about 200 pupils, and a Catholic gymnasium with 19 professors and about 350 pupils; and was formerly the seat of a university founded in 1385, but suppressed in 1797. C. is favourably situated for trade, forming an intermediate point between Germany and Holland; and its commerce, in corn, wine, rape-seed, and coals, particularly in Rhenish wine, or hock, is very considerable. Its trade in cloth, linen, lace, cotton, and silk, tobacco and earthenware, is also still important; and it is celebrated for the distillation of Kölnische Wasser or Eau de Cologne, of which many thousand bottles are exported every year by the various rival manufacturers of this article. A considerable quantity of brandy is also made. As a great city, where magazines can be conveniently established, and military provisions obtained,—as a convenient place for crossing the Rhine,—as an intermediate point between Wesel and Coblenz, and as constituting a part of the basis from which must proceed the operations of German armies against the Netherlands and France, C. is of great military importance. Its fortifications were restored in 1815; and have been strengthened by a chain of casemated towers, each of which contains several stories and has a few cannon. These are placed at some distance from the city, as separate and detached works. C. has thus become a strong place, though not, indeed, so important a fortress as Coblenz. The small city of Deutz, on the r. bank of the Rhine, opposite C., is likewise fortified, and thus completes the double tete-de-pont. A bridge resting on 39 pontoons, and 1,250 paces in length, at present connects the two sides of the river; but the Prussian minister of trade and public works has issued a public notice inviting the engineers of all nations to send in plans of a fixed bridge at C., to unite the lines of railway between Belgium and France with the great German line to Vienna. The river from bank to bank is stated in this official notice to be 1,275 Rhenish ft. wide; this space must be crossed by a bridge leaving three openings; the piers are to occupy in all not more than 75 ft., and must be so firmly built as to stand the pressure of the fields of ice that descend the stream on the break-up of great frosts. The bridge must support a tramway for loaded railway waggons, a roadway for ordinary carriages, and footpaths. Locomotives will not pass it, nor unbroken trains, and passengers will be taken across from terminus to terminus. The communication therefore will still be imperfect, but it is necessary to obtain a certain height above the water to meet the immense rise of the river in floods; and, as the termini on the respective banks are on a low level, the ascent from them to the roadway of the bridge will be too short and steep for locomotives. The bridge crosses the river from the N side of the C. and Minden station at Deutz, in nearly a straight line, drawn towards the choir of the cathedral immediately opposite. In the design some attention is to be paid to this circumstance, in order that the bridge in exterior effect may be worthy its position. The cost is not to ex- COL panying cut the present boat-bridge is at a a a; the projected new bridge will cross the river nearly at b b b. The site of the cathedral is at D; of the Gereons-Driesch at G; of the Neumarkt at N; of the present terminus of the Minden railway, in the suburb of Deutz, at m. The Severon-thor, Weyerthor, Hahnen-thor, Ehren-thor, Gereons-thor, and Eigelstein-thor, are respectively numbered, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. In 1840 there arrived at C. by the Rhine, navigating upwards, 1,508 vessels, carrying 1,708,607 quintals of merchandise, and, navigating downwards, 4,776 vessels, carrying 1,465,514 quintals. In 1841 there arrived 1,638 vessels, and 2,079,760 quintals, upwards; and 5,133 vessels, and 1,573,062 quintals, downwards. In 1840 there departed from C., upwards, 1,784 vessels, with 682,173 quintals of merchandise, and, downwards, 938 vessels, with 321,839 quintals. In 1841 there departed, upwards, 1,927 vessels, with 880,785 quintals, and, downwards, 1,097 vessels, with 396,135 quintals. The total movement of the arrivals and departures was, in 1840, 9,005 vessels, and 4,178,133 quintals of merchandise; in 1841, 9,777 vessels, and 4,917,742 quintals. The steam-boat navigation has had a large share in this general movement of the navigation of the Rhine to C. Thus, in 1841, there were 3,210 arrivals and departures of steam-vessels, which transported 676,462 quintals of merchandise. —C. owes its origin to the Romans. It was the Colonia Claudia Agrippinensis of that people, and the cap. of Lower Rhenish Gaul till 330. In 870 it was annexed to the German empire. In the early part of the 14th cent. C. was called 'the Rome of the North.' It was at that time the principal town of the great Hanseatic league, and its pop. amounted to 150,000. It was the birth-place of the celebrated painter Rubens; and it was here that the monk Barthold Schwarz invented gunpowder in 1330.

COLOMBIA, a now dismembered republic of South America, which, until its recent dissolution, was composed of those districts that under Spanish domination were known by the names of the captaingeneralcy of Venezuela, and the vice-royalty of New Granada, lying between 12° 25' N lat. and 6° 15' S lat. The Caribbean sea, from Punta-Careta to the Morocco and Pamarous rivers, formed the boundary of the federal states on the N; on the E and SE they were bounded by British, Dutch, and French Guayana; on the S by Portuguese Guayana, and by Peru. From Peru, Colombia was divided by a line drawn SE from the river Tumbez, in S lat. 3° 30', across the Andes, till it strikes the Tunguragua, in 6° S lat., a little to the S of Jaen-de-Bracamoros; thence NE along this stream, as far as the pongo, or narrow pass, where the Tunguragua issues from the western face of the Andes; thence to the confluence of that river with the Apurimac or Maranon, and down this mighty stream to the W mouth of the Yapura. The Pacific ocean formed its boundary on the W, along an extent of 11° of latitude. The extent of its coasts exceeded 3,000 m.; and its total superficies probably amounted to 1,017,000 sq. m., being an area 12 times larger than the island of Great Britain, and nearly equal to the extent of the whole territory of the United States E of the Mississippi. Civil divisions.] The territory of this republic, before its partition, was successively divided into 7, 10, and 12 departments. These departments were unequally subdivided into provinces; the provinces into cantons; and the cantons into parishes. History.] The earlier history of this part of Spanish America presents few events of sufficient importance to attract the attention of general readers. An attempt was made by the Scots to establish a settlement on the isthmus of Darien, in 1698; which, had it not been thwarted and ruined by the mean and selfish policy of a few monied monopolists, might ultimately have proved of vast advantage, political and commercial, to Great Britain. Paterson, a Scots clergyman, was the author of this illfated project, which was patronized by Fletcher of Salton, and adopted through his influence by the Scottish ministry. In a short time nearly £1,000,000 was subscribed towards this adventure in England, Scotland, and Holland; of which sum £400,000 was raised in Scotland alone, being one-half of the cash then in the kingdom. Two expeditions, amounting together to 2,500 men, successively sailed from Leith for the projected settlement; but, before the second expedition arrived, the first adventurers had either died, or left the new settlement in despair, in consequence of orders issued by the English and Dutch governments to their respective colonies, prohibiting them from all correspondence with the infant colony. Cut off from supplies, -interdicted all communication with the West Indies or with British America, -and blockaded both by sea and land,-this ill-fated colony was obliged to capitulate to the Spaniards. From the time of the conquest and settlement of Caraccas, down to the commencement of the present century, the annals of this country present no events of importance, with the exception of the occasional inroads of fierce and inhuman buccaneers. Like the rest of the Spanish colonies, that of Caraccas long shared in feelings of dissatisfaction towards the mother-country; and these feelings were strengthened and confirmed by the successful example of the British colonies in North America. The Spanish-Americans complained, 1st, of the arbitrary power exercised by the viceroys and captains-general, who frequently evaded the laws, and even the orders which they received from Spain. 2d. That the audiencias were composed solely of Europeans, who interpreted the laws as they pleased. 3d. That under the authority of the audiencias, clandestine decrees in causes were often made; nocturnal arrests took place; parties were banished without previous trial; and numerous other acts of injustice were committed. 4th. That they-the native Spanish- Americans—were treated with distrust by the government, notwithstanding the loyalty and courage which upon several occasions they had manifested in defence of the rights of the crown of Spain. 5th. They likewise complained that they were obliged to bear insults from the meanest Spaniards, who, merely upon account of their European birth, considered themselves their superiors. 6th. They complained that notwithstanding the original compact made between the king and the first settlers in Spanish America, which stipulated "that in all cases of government, justice, administration of finances, commissions, &c., the first discoverers, then the pacificadores, and lastly, the settlers, and those born in the said provinces, were to be preferred in all appointments and public employments,"-they, the Creoles, were gradually shut out from all participation in local commands and dignities; that they were also prohibited from visiting the mother-country, without the express permission of the Crown, which could not be obtained but with much difficulty. 7th. That the South Americans were prohibited from making wine or brandy, or extracting oils, and from planting vines or almond trees, except in Peru or Chili, or from cultivating more than a specified and limited number of tobacco-plants; whilst the wine, almonds, &c., produced in Peru and Chili, were not permitted to be sent to Mexico, New Granada, or Terra Firma; and it was forbidden to cultivate tobacco or the sugar-cane in Chili. 8th. That in order to check the progress of population, and to keep distinct the different classes, there were many laws tending to throw obstacles in the way of marriages. In 1797, the flame had nearly burst forth with irresistible violence. Don Josef Espana, a rich merchant of Caraccas, and Don Manuel Wal, an officer of engineers residing at Guayra, conceived the bold design of establishing the independence of Venezuela, and uniting to it the provinces of Cumana, Maracaibo, Varinas, and Guayana, under the name of the United States of South America; but the confederates were arrested before the general insurrection could take place. Espana, brought to the scaffold, died with Roman firmness; and Wal finished his existence in the isle of Trinidad. A few years after this, Miranda, a native of Caraccas, who, like Kosciusko and La Fayette, had served in the independent army during the American war, and afterwards in the French army, landed in Venezuela with a handful of adventurers whom he had picked up in the United States. His means, however, were so inadequate, and his measures so injudicious, that not a man joined him; and after taking possession of Coro, he found it necessary in a few days to re-embark, leaving such of his followers as had fallen into the hands of the Spaniards to suffer as pirates, without making a single effort to liberate them or to mitigate their fate. At the moment when Spain was invaded by Bonaparte, the spirit of revolt began again to show itself in Caraccas. No sooner had the news arrived of the overthrow of the Spanish armies, the fall of Seville, and the retreat of the junta to the Isle of Leon, than the disaffected party, having previously gained over the military, seized the governor, Emparan, as he was entering the cathedral to celebrate mass, on holy Thursday, the 19th of April, 1811,—forced him to the consistory, where the council were assembled,—and there compelled him to resign his government. The governor, and the other members of the administration, were sent to the coast, and shipped off; and a supreme Junta was immediately formed, which began its career by decrees and proclamations,—took off the alcavala from all articles of food or of first necessity,—exempted the Indians from the capitation tax,—threw open the prisons,—and bribed the military by promising them double pay. At Barcelona, all opposition to the revolution was instantly crushed; and the revolutionary cause enthusiastically embraced. This example was followed through the greater part of the captaincy; Coro, Maracaibo, Valencia, and Guayana, alone dissenting. Commissioners were immediately sent after these proceedings to England and to the United States. The regency of Cadiz, as soon as they were informed of the proceedings of the Junta, declared the Caraccas in a state of rebellion, ordered its ports to be blockaded, and sent over a deputy to attempt to pacify the insurgents; but he was not suffered to land, and his proposals of reconciliation with the mother-country were contemptuously rejected. The Junta,—who had entitled themselves 'the Supreme conservative Junta of Venezuela,'-encouraged by a letter from Miranda advising them to have no connexion with Spain, invited that adventurer to join their cause, and erased all the records of former proceedings against him. A congress was summoned from all the provinces of the captaincy. This congress, to which Miranda himself was returned a deputy, assembled on the 1st of July, 1811, and its first act was a solemn renunciation of allegiance to Ferdinand VII. On the publication of this manifesto, many individuals who held public offices resigned them,—others retired from the country,-while the old Spaniards and Canarians entered into conspiracies against the new government. A new constitution was next framed by the congress; among the chief articles of which were, the abolition of torture,-provision for trial by jury,-the prohibition of the slave-trade,—the non-toleration of any religion except the Roman Catholic, which was declared to be the one sole and exclusive code of religious faith of the whole commuity, -and the placing of the legislative and executive functions in the hands of three magistrates, who were to hold their power for four years. The proceedings of congress were, however, soon disturbed by the interference of what was denominated a patriotic society founded in the city of Caraccas, six months after the arrival of Miranda, who was declared its president. This society -like its prototype the Jacobin club in Paris-met regularly to discuss political subjects. Several Frenchmen were members of it; and deputations from it regularly appeared before the bar of the congress, and pronounced philippies against the British as tyrants of the sea. In the meantime, notwithstanding the execution of most of the royalists who had prematurely ventured to conspire against the new order of things, several parts of the captaincy still adhered to the mother-country. Against these an armed force was sent, under the Marquis del Toro; but the royalists of Coro beat him back with considerable loss. On the side of Guayana, the revolutionists were equally unfortunate; their flotilla on the Orinoco being defeated; while Miranda had no better success against the city of Valencia. But in three weeks after, Miranda, reinforced to upwards of 5,000 men, again attacked and captured Valencia, after having hemmed in the royalists, and starved them to a surrender, on the 18th of August, 1811. Coro and Maracaibo, however, remained unreduced; and Miranda began to be checked in his career by causes which might have been foreseen. In a country so thinly peopled, and without manufactures, soldiers could not be provided without taking them from the toils of agriculture; while the new government raised soldiers by force, the crops were left to perish on the ground for want of hands to gather them. The consequences of this state of things were soon felt in the treasury; and the people began to grow discontented. Miranda, hailed at first as a deliverer, was, notwithstanding his successes, denounced to the executive as a tyrant. An awful and unforeseen catastrophe terminated the career of Miranda, and for a season placed the captaincy of Venezuela in complete possession of the royalists: this was the earthquake of 1812 Immediately after its occurrence, the desolated cities hastened to send in their submission to Monteverte, the royalist commander, who advanced towards the capital, Caraccas, while the congress, as a last remedy, made Miranda dictator, who proclaimed martial law. But the Venezuelans were persuaded that the wrath of the Almighty had kindled against them; and Miranda's army deserted so fast that he found himself compelled to surrender without fighting. Some of his chief adherents got on ship-board, and escaped to the United States; and Monteverte, contrary to the terms of the capitulation, made use of this circumstance as a pretext for sending him and a few of the other leaders prisoners to Spain. Such was the termination of this adventurer's career, who died after a rigorous confinement of four years, in the prison of La Cabarca, near Cadiz. Venezuela now acknowledged the regency and the Cortes; and Spain had an opportunity of retrieving her past errors by a mild and conciliatory conduct. But this invaluable season was lost. The royalists, in all the insolence of triumph, conducted themselves with a degree of cruelty and imprudence which could not fail of again ruining their cause. "Thus," says Cochrane, "in the first Cortes which assembled at Cadiz, the Spanish members, whilst they were eagerly anxious to obtain the freedom of their own country, seemed equally solicitous, in the same breath, to enslave the Spanish Americans. Some of them even betrayed a malignity which, impolitic as it was, to say the least of it, considering that they were speaking in the presence and hearing of the American members who formed part of the Cortes, was rendered supremely ridiculous by its impotence. It was said by one, 'If the Americans complain of having been tyrannized over for 300 years, they shall now experience a similar treatment for 3,000.' By another it was said, after the battle of Albufera, 'I am rejoiced at the advantage we have gained, because we can now send troops to reduce the insurgents;' by a third, 'I do not know to what class of beasts the Americans belong." These insolent vauntings were speedily shown to be premature. Don Simon Bolivar, who had been one of the deputies from the Caraccas to England in 1810, having raised an army of 1,000 men, after several successful battles with the royalists, entered the city of Caraccas, on the 4th of August, 1813. He continued successfully to attack and defeat the remaining forces of the royalists; and having offered his resignation as military commander into the hands of a representative assembly, was by it formally appointed sole dictator. In order to recover their superiority, the Spaniards had now recourse to the desperate measure of a general liberation of the slaves. By this means they raised an army, or rather an armed rabble, of above 70,000 men, and defeated Bolivar in a general action, at 150 m. distance from Caraccas; but the latter was soon in the field again, and renewed the struggle with greater success. The same spirit of dissatisfaction which existed in Mexico, and in general throughout almost all Spanish America, had long reigned in New Granada. No sooner had the Caraccas declared for independence, than the viceroy of Santa Fé prohibited, under the severest penalties, all communication with the revolted provinces. But his precautions and proclamations were alike vain. The imprudent and violent conduct of the royalist party in the viceroyalty, roused the latent elements of disaffection into flame. The corregidor of Socorro, by causing the troops under his command to fire upon a mutinous but unarmed populace, exasperated them into fury, and an insurrection took place. Attacked by an iminto a surrender, on the 9th of July 1810. Socorro immediately the regency as the legitimate representative of Ferdinand in Europe; but in the meantime another event occurred, which increased all the former jealousies and apprehensions entertained by the Creoles, respecting the officers and servants of the mother country: this was the conduct of the royalists respecting Quito. That city having joined the general defection, was besieged and captured by a body of troops belonging to the viceroy of Lima, who put to death one out of every five of the inhabitants by whom the city had been defended. Upon the intelligence of this massacre reaching Santa Fé, the viceroy was instantly deprived of his authority; and in the spring of 1811, a general congress was held at Santa-Fé-de-Bogota, which abjured the provisional government of Spain, but acknowledged Ferdinand as the lawful king and sovereign of Cundinamarca, the new appellation they had given to New Grenada. The congress of Cundinamarca appointed Nurino general of their forces; who was for some time victorious, but was taken prisoner in June 1814. The congress, however, not dispirited, employed Bolivar to compel the province of Cundinamarca, with Santa Fé the capital, to submit to their form of government: in this object he succeeded, and the congress proceeded again to hold their sittings in that city. Murillo shortly after arrived with a body of 10,000 troops from Spain, tured Santa Fé, after a spirited resistance from the independents; but the latter, after a series of unparalleled struggles, ultimately prevailed under the guidance of Bolivar. New Granada and Venezuela were united under one government by a decree of congress assembled at Angostura, on December 11, 1819. The new government assumed the title of 'the Republic of Colombia;' and on the 6th of December, 1821, a congress-general, consisting of deputies from all the states within the present boundaries of Colombia, was enabled to assemble at Rosario-de-Cucuta, which promulgated a federal constitution on the 30th of August, 1823. In 1828, Bolivar assumed supreme power; the republic was disturbed by violent factions, and, in 1829, Venezuela separated from it. In 1830 a general convention met at Bogota, to frame a new constitution; Bolivar resigned, and took leave of public life; on the 4th of May, 1830, Senor Joachim Mosquera was elected president; and General Domingo Caicedo, vice-president; but on the 4th of September, Mosquera resigned, and Urdanata was appointed temporary president, until the arrival of Bolivar, whose return to power was decreed by a meeting of soldiers and citizens; but he died at Carthagena, on the 17th of Dec. of the same year. Venezuela again joined Colombia for a short time; but in November, 1831, a new separation took place, and since that time the republic of Colombia has been divided into three republics, New Granada, Venezuela, and Equator or Ecuador, whose constitutions are similar to that framed for Colombia, as above narrated, at Rosario-de-Cucuta. COLOMBO, or COLUMBO, the capital of the European establishments in Ceylon, placed nearly in the middle of the W coast, in N lat. 6° 57', E long. 79° 50'; 368 m. SW of Madras. A very fertile district, about 60 m. in length, and 30 m. in breadth, lies between the city and the border of the central tableland, formerly the limit of the Singhalese empire. A peninsula, stretching out into the sea, and united to the mainland by an isthmus not 600 yards broad, is nearly covered by the fort, which, from its exposure to the sea-breezes, always enjoys a temperate and healthy atmosphere. This fortress-an irregular octagon, having five of its fronts washed by the sea, and the remainder towards the land covered by a lakewas erected by the Portuguese on the ruins of a smaller one built by the natives. Its position is strong by nature, and its works have been improved by the labours of Dutch engineers. It mounts 126 guns, and 6 mortars. A high surf and rocky beach render any approach impossible on the S, and nu- W side of the bay, where there is usually smooth water. Its works, however, to be manned effectively, would require a garrison of 6,000 men. The centre of the narrow isthmus connecting this peninsula with the rest of the island, is occupied by a lake extending 3 or 4 m. into the neighbouring country, so that there is only a narrow causeway left on each side of it, and the site of the fort might be easily insulated. The town within the fort is regularly built, having all its streets at right angles; two, which are spacious, extend from side to side quite across the town, and divide the whole into four nearly equal quarters. A broadway at the foot of the ramparts, communicating with the bastions and barracks, leaves open areas for parades. The houses are generally one story high, and built in the Dutch style, with a porch or corridor in front. 'The Queen's house,' or governor's house, facing the harbour, is a long range of buildings, more remarkable for convenience than beauty. Nearly opposite it are the Ceylon and Oriental banks, which completely eclipse it in appearance. The Dutch church, in which the service is performed in Singhalese, is very handsome. There is also a neat Wesleyan Methodist chapel, and an English church called St. Peter's. The Malabar and Portuguese Protestants have each their church. The Moors have two handsome mosques; and the Hindus have several temples. A library, a medical museum, and an hospital, have been established within the fort. Some handsome shops have been opened by Europeans; and there are now upwards of 20 commercial houses in C. The water in the wells within the walls is uniformly brackish, and a spring about 1 m. off is the nearest fresh water that can be procured: of this almost all the inhabitants have a supply brought in daily, in leathern bags carried by bullocks. C. has more of an European appearance than almost any other place in the East Indies; and the double rows of tulip (Hibiscus Zeilanicus) and bread-fruit trees which line the principal streets, are an ornament and convenience for which the inhabitants are probably indebted to the Dutch. The chunam, or shell-lime, with which the houses are white-washed, is peculiarly white, and contributes materially to keep them cool, though it is disagreeable and injurious to the eyes. The Pettah, or Black town, on the N side of the fort, is densely populated, and is remarkable for its extent, regularity and neatness. One part of it extends from the esplanade to the Kenman's-port, a gate in an old mud rampart; and the other, outside of the wall, stretches along the isthmus already mentioned to 3 or 4 m. from the fort. Most of the principal European settlers have their habitations in the former of these divisions; the latter contains the orphan school, an excellent institution, where the children of indigent Europeans, especially soldiers and sailors, are educated and put out to some useful trade or service. There are several bazaars in the Pettah for fish, flesh, fruits, grain, and vegetables. It is difficult to estimate the number of the inhabitants; for besides the fixed residents, there is always a large floating population, collected from almost every country in Europe and Asia; and there are few places where the stranger is amused by such a continual variety of dress, features, habits, and languages. Moguls and Hindus from all the widely scattered provinces of India; Malays and Bugis from the Indian isles; Parsis and Shiahs, the worshippers of fire, and partisans of Ali, from Persia; and various tribes of savages from Mozambique and Madagascar, are to be seen on the streets of C.; and to these must be added the half-castes, the progeny of Europeans of almost every nation, but chiefly of the Portuguese,—a corrupt and wretched dialect of whose language is still the lingua Franca of this country. This motley assemblage was estimated COL by Cordiner at 50,000 souls. In 1832 it was returned at 31,519.—Its convenient position with respect to the coast of India, and the fertility of the neighbouring districts, render C. a sort of emporium for the produce of Ceylon, and merchandise imported from other parts of the world. The largest and most valuable plantations of cinnamon are 'the gardens,' as the Dutch term them, in this district, though the word is a misnomer, for there is nothing in the mode of planting and arranging the cinnamon trees to realize the ideas conveyed by the word. They commence within half-a-mile of the fort. The principal garden stretches over an extent of country from 10 m. to 15 m. in length. The flat lands between the sea and hills are covered with the low cinnamon-laurel, over which, at an inconsiderable distance, rise groves of evergreens, interspersed with clumps of palms, and occasionally with forests of lofty trees. Small lakes, rice-grounds, and pastures, are scattered over the whole space, through which an excellent road makes a circuit of 7 m. from the W to the S gate of the fort, and affords a delightful drive for the wealthier inhabitants. A canal, constructed by the Dutch, connects the fort with the Kalané-ganga, the chief river of the island, which debouches at Modera, 4 m. to the N of C.—A railway has been projected from C. to Kandy. See article CEYLON, p. 430.—The port of C. extends from that of Caltura on the one hand to that of Negombo on the other; but it is only to the outer and the inner harbour at C. itself that shipping resort at present. The harbours are separated by a bar formed by the river Kalany, which lies across the bay, and has only about 12 ft. of water over it. The outer harbour is a safe anchorage at all seasons. But from the middle of May till the middle of July, when the SW monsoon is most violent, and again from the 1st December till the end of January, when the long shore-wind prevails, transport of goods between the shipping and the shore is sometimes liable to be interrupted for several days at a time. The most suitable season for delivering and taking in cargo is from the 1st of February till the end of April, which is happily also the season when the greatest quantity of coffee arrives from the interior. The inner harbour is chiefly the resort of native craft and small country brigs, the former from October till April only, the latter all the year. It is safe all the year round, although during the long shorewind care must be taken to prevent getting foul or ashore. C. is mentioned in Singhalese history as early as 495. In 1374 it was frequented by trading vessels, and a colony of Malabars attempted to get possession of it. The Portuguese arrived here in 1510. Pridham says, the word colamba signifies in Singhalese 'a seaport,' or 'a fort;' and thinks that this may have been corrupted into Colombo by the Portuguese in honour of their celebrated navigator.

COLOMERA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 12 m. N of Granada, partido of Iznalloz, on a hill near the Rio Lachena. Pop. 2,200. It contains a parish-church, an hospital, a bank, and a custom-house. Agriculture forms the chief branch of local industry.
COLOMIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. and 6 m. W of Toulouse. Pop. 1,149.

COLON, an island of Spain, in the Baleares group, near the E coast of the island of Minorca, at the entrance of the port of Bufera, in N lat. 39° 58'.—Also a town and port on the SE coast of the island of Majorca, 35 m. ESE of Palma, in N lat. 39° 25'.
COLON, a township of St. Joseph co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., 135 m. WSW of Detroit. Pop. in 1840, 420.
COLONA-DI-BURIANO, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Grosseto, 10 m. NE of Castiglion-della-Pescaja. Pop. 283. It contains numerous remains of Roman and Etruscan antiquities.
COLONCHE, a town of New Grenada, in the intendancy and 50 m. WNW of Guayaquil, on a river which flows into Santa Elena bay.

COLONELLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 19 m. NNE of Teramo, near the r. bank of the Tronte, and 3 m. from the Adriatic. Pop. 2,000. It contains 5 churches, a convent, and a school. Fairs are held twice a-year.
COLONGE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Correze, cant. of Meissac, 10 m. SE of Brives. Pop. 1,183.

COLONI, a village of Central Africa, in the kingdom of Jenné. It is situated in a fertile plain, and is surrounded by two mud walls. The pop., amounting to about 400, consists of Foulahs, Bambaras, and Mandingoes.—Caillie.
COLONIA-DEL-SACRAMENTO, or Colonia-

DO-SANTISSIMO-SACRAMENTO, a fortified town and port of Uruguay, on the l. bank of the estuary of the La Plata, nearly opposite Buenos Ayres, and 100 m. WNW of Monte Video. Pop. 2,000. It possesses a considerable trade in cattle, wool, and salted meat.

COLONNA (CAPE), a headland of Greece,—the Sunium Promontorium of the ancients,—at the SE extremity of Attica, in N lat. 37° 39′ 12", E long. 24° 2'. It rises precipitously from the sea; and derives its present name from a range of 16 white marble columns, the remains of a temple of Minerva Sunias, which stand on its summit, and which are visible from a considerable distance at sea. Its scenery has been "classic in all ages." Early travellers remarked that the shining white marble columns presented an agreeable contrast to the dark green foliage of the trees surrounding the temple; but nothing now remains of such an enclosure. The still beautiful and imposing relics of "Tritonia's sacred fane" stand quite freely upon "the Cape's projecting verge," which is covered only with low shrubs.
COLONNA (CAPE), a headland on the S coast of the island of Samos, at the mouth of the Imbrasius, to the SW of the ruins of the ancient town of Samos, and opposite the small island of Samo Poulo.

COLONNE (CAPO DELLE), or CAPE NAN, a promontory of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, near the entrance of the gulf of Tarento, in N lat. 39° 5'. It is the Lacinium promontorium of the ancients, so called from the temple dedicated to Juno Lacinia, the ruins of which are still to be seen on its summit.
COLONSAY. See Collonsay.
COLOPE, a river of New Grenada, in the prov. of Imbabura, which flows into the Pacific, 10 m. E of the embouchure of the Esmeraldas.

COLORA'DO, a large river of Upper California, falling into the N extremity of the gulf of California after a course of nearly 800 m. It rises in the S pass of the Rocky mountains, under the parallel of 42° 20' N. The elevation of this pass is about 7,000 ft. above sea-level; yet the ascent to it from the Platte and Sweetwater rivers, on the E side, over a sandy plain 120 m. long, is so gradual that the traveller is scarcely conscious of rising to the summit of a high ridge of mountains. After passing 2 or 3 m. over a level surface, between low and sloping elevations composed of sand and clay, he descends by a gentle declivity 2 m. to a spring known to emigrants as the 'Pacific spring,' the water from which forms the upper stream of the C., known to travellers and trappers as the Green river. The distance from Fort | Valle-de-Flores through a solid block resembling Laramie to the Pacific spring is 311 m. [Bryant.] marble, and then breaks into a perpendicular fall of COL Within the space of a few miles, the Wind mountains supply a number of tributaries to the Green river. At a point in about 40° 46' N lat., and at an elevation of 5,150 ft. above sea-level, the C., in the month of June, was found by Fremont and his party to be swollen to the top of its banks, and several hundred yards in breadth, with a depth in many places near its banks of from 15 to 20 ft. Below this, the river enters between lofty precipices of red rock, and the country is said to assume a very rugged character, the river and its affluents passing through canons which forbid all access to its verge. The course of the C. is prevailingly SSW. It is joined on the l. by the Nabajoa under the 37th parallel; and by the Jaquesila about 40 m. lower down. Dr. Lyman describes it as flowing through a country perfectly barren, and with little or no vegetation. See also article California, p. 196. Under the parallel of 32° 30′, the Gila comes into it nearly at right angles; and the point of junction, strangely chosen, is the hard butte, through which, with their united forces, they cut a canon 1,000 ft. in length, with vertical walls about 50 ft. high, and then flow off due W in a stream 600 ft. wide, and with a perfectly straight course, for 3 or 4 m. Emory is of opinion that up to its junction with the Gila, in N lat. 32° 43', the C. is always navigable for steam-boats; above, it is full of shifting sand-bars. From this point the wide bed of the C. is overgrown thickly with mezquite, willows, Equisetum hyemale, and cotton-wood. At the ford leading to Sonora, about 70 m. from its mouth, the C. was found to be 1,500 ft. wide, and flowing at the rate of 11 m. per hour, in the end of November. Its banks were low, and its greatest depth at the crossing was only 4 ft.

COLORADO (Rio), or Cobu Leubu, a large river of La Plata, rising in the Andes, under the parallel of 36° S, and flowing in a SE course to the Atlantic, which it enters in S lat. 39° 51', W long. 62° 04', after a course of perhaps 900 m. through a country almost entirely unknown. Where crossed by Mr. Darwin, at a point 9 leagues direct distance from its mouth, it was only 60 yards wide, in the month of August; but he is of opinion that generally it must be nearly double that width. Its course, marked by willow-trees and beds of reeds, was very tortuous below this point, the distance by water to the mouth of the river being 25 leagues. At its mouth the river divides into numerous streams, forming small islands, which are covered with rushes. The principal mouth has 2 fath, water when the tide is out, but is beset to seaward by sand-banks which shift with every SE gale. The tide rises here from 6 to 9 ft.

COLORADO, a river of Texas, which rises in the extreme W part of the country, in the Guadalupe mountains, from which it has a course to the sea of about 600 m., running first in a NE direction to its junction with the Pasigono, whence it inclines to the SE. Its banks are steep and seldom overflowed, and abound in fine timber. About 10 m. from its mouth is 'the raft,' formed of drifted wood, which greatly obstructs the navigation. It consists of detached masses of timber, with portions of the river intervening. The C. enters the bay of Matagorda by two outlets, about 2 m. apart. The following creeks enter the C. from the E: Cumming's, Rabb's, Pine, Oak, Eblin's, Walberger's, Bear, Hamilton, Cypress, Honey, and Hunting. From the W it receives Wilson's, Jenning's, Jones', Buckner's, Cedar, Walnut, Onion, Spring, and Bull creeks. The Agua-Fria enters the C. from the W, 12 m. above Austin. The stream runs for 600 yards from its source in the 20 ft. Nearly 22 m. above Austin, the Piedernales, | or Flint river, enters the C. from the W. Although its length does not exceed 70 m., it is so much increased by tributary waters from mountain-springs, that it rivals the main stream in volume near the point of confluence. The Llano also flows from the W, and carries a considerable body of water to the C. A few miles above the mouth of the Llano, and 4 m. below Hunting-creek, are the great Falls of the C.,—a succession of cascades extending about 800 yds. The first fall is about 20 ft. perpendicular. Within 100 yds. there is another of 10 ft. And thus they continue until they reach a total perpendicular fall of nearly 100 ft. The C. flows with undiminished size and an untroubled current for about 200 m. above the Falls. The San-Saba, a river about 200 m. in length, rises in a spur of the Guadalupe mountains, and winds throughout its NNE course between two hilly ridges, almost to the margin of the C. For 40 m. above its confluence with the C., the San-Saba is about 50 yds. wide, and generally deep, but it is obstructed by numerous shoals. The Pecan-Bayou, which enters the C. about 25 m. above the mouth of the San-Saba, has a course of 120 m. in length. About 100 m. above the mouth of the Pecan-Bayou, the Pasigono river enters the C. from the NE. Its tributaries are the Piaroya, the Muchique, and the Salado-creeks. Above the mouth of the Pasigono, the principal branches of the C. are the Pisape-Junova, Arroyo-Frio, and the Aguilas.-The general appearance of the C. is similar to the Thames from Chelsea to Richmond, but it is perhaps rather larger than the English river. During the dry season, at low water, its average depth may be from 6 to 8 ft., except at the shoals. When 'the Raft' has been removed, and the contemplated improvements have been completed, it is anticipated that the C. will be navigable by small steamers of a suitable construction, as high as Austin, or about 220 m. above its embouchure.

COLORADO, an arm of the river San-Juan, in Central America. It parts from the main stream at a point about 12 m. distant from the sea, and, fetching a circuit southwards into the territory of Costarica, flows into the ocean a few miles to the S of the central deltoid branch of the San-Juan. It is reported that the force of the stream of the San-Juan has of late years taken the direction of this branch; and that the other deltoid branches are encumbered with islands and sand-banks. See San-Juan.—Also a river of New Grenada, in the dep. of Santa-Marta, which flows into the Magdalena, on the r. bank, at Tamalameque, after a W course of about 65 m.
COLORADO, or Cahorra, a volcanic mountain in the island of Teneriffe, to the SW of the peak of that name.
COLORADO (CERRO), a mountain range in the La Plata prov. of Tucuman, intersected by the parallel of 26° N, about 60 m. W of the Rio Salado.

COLORADOS, a village of Bolivia, on the road from Humaguaca in the La Plata prov. of Salta, to Suipicha in Bolivia, on the N declivity of the Alrade-Cortaderas, 85 m. SSW of Tarija.—Also a town in the La Plata prov. of Rioja, on the route from Cordova to Rioja, or to Chilecito, 85 m. S by W of Rioja.
COLORADOS (Los), a group of reefs and kays at the entrance of the gulf of Mexico, off the NW coast of the island of Cuba. Their SW extremity lies in N lat. 22° 19', W long. 84° 45'.
